symptoms.symptom_name: Thick greasy yellow-brown crusting on the scalp vertex

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Greasy yellow scalp scale (cradle cap) in early infancy is the hallmark of seborrhoeic dermatitis.
Atopic eczema is typically dry and ill-defined on the cheeks/extensors, not greasy yellow scalp scale.

symptoms.symptom_name: Poorly-marginated erythematous plaques with greasy yellow scale over forehead, eyebrows and nasolabial folds (seborrhoeic distribution), sparing cheeks and limbs

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Involvement of the greasy seborrhoeic sites with sparing of the cheeks/extensors is characteristic and distinguishes it from atopic dermatitis.
Atopic eczema favours the cheeks and extensor surfaces in infancy, the opposite emphasis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Post-auricular intertriginous erythema with serous weeping and a small fissure that bled with rubbing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Flexural (post-auricular) involvement with maceration is a recognised feature of infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis.
Weeping, fissured, macerated skin is a portal of entry for bacteria and must be screened for infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset ~4 weeks ago, initially mild scalp crusting, with worsening and spread over the last 48 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A subacute course beginning in the first months of life fits the natural history of infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Parental application of thick occlusive natural oils (olive/coconut) to the scalp

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Occlusive oils increase substrate and occlusion, promoting Malassezia overgrowth and aggravating the eruption; identifying and stopping them is the key modifiable driver.

symptoms.symptom_name: No thick yellow/green pus, no honey-coloured crusting, no spreading hot indurated cellulitic edge, no fluctuance

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Serous (not purulent/honey-coloured) weeping and absence of a spreading indurated edge make active impetigo/cellulitis unlikely today, though not fully excludable remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile on home thermometer (36.8C), no vomiting, well-perfused and breathing comfortably

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever, vomiting, poor perfusion or respiratory distress makes systemic sepsis very unlikely and supports a benign local dermatosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No petechiae/purpura within the rash, normal growth and weight gain until this episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of petechiae/purpura and failure to thrive makes LCH unlikely, though a treatment-resistant course would warrant review.

symptoms.symptom_name: Moderate-to-severe pruritus with the infant rubbing the head against bedding

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Itch and friction perpetuate the maceration but are non-specific; they contribute to the weeping and superficial bleeding.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced feeding and inconsolable irritability in a young infant

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Discomfort-related reduced feeding is expected with a severe flare, but poor feeding in an infant is also a serious-illness cue and must be tracked as a safety-net trigger if it worsens.

symptoms.symptom_name: Infant vital signs (HR 145, RR 35, SpO2 99%) obtained by the clinician

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal-for-age observations in a crying infant are reassuring; these are clinician-measured and sealed — the AI Doctor infers wellbeing from caregiver-observable colour, breathing and behaviour, not measured vitals.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Clinical (visual) diagnosis — no laboratory or imaging investigation required for typical infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The diagnosis is clinical; routine bloods, imaging or skin biopsy are not indicated and constitute over-investigation of a benign dermatosis.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Bacterial swab (MC&S) of the weeping post-auricular skin
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Only if secondary bacterial infection is suspected in person (pus, spreading erythema, systemic signs) — guides targeted antibiotic therapy; not needed on today's screened-negative findings.
Reserved for a treatment-resistant or atypical seborrhoeic-like eruption to exclude Langerhans cell histiocytosis — not indicated at first presentation with a typical picture.
